ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general mushroom soup class (FOODON_00002539); no specific class exists for cashew-truffle mushroom soup served in a bread bowl. Closest parent match used; bread bowl component is not captured in the ontology.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers soups and broths as a category. No specific code exists for cashew truffle mushroom soup bread bowl. Closest parent category-level match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No defensible SNOMED CT code identified for this composite dish. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is a hollowed-out bread bowl (approximately 550 g total) filled with creamy cashew truffle mushroom soup, providing roughly 650 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Mushroom soup, cream of, canned, condensed (SR Legacy ID 06037) and Bread, white, commercially prepared (SR Legacy ID 18069); component-weighted estimation for composite dish
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for cashew truffle mushroom soup bread bowl as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by component weighting: cream of mushroom soup (USDA SR Legacy 06037) adjusted for cashew cream substitution (USDA SR Legacy 12087 — raw cashews) and bread bowl component (USDA SR Legacy 18069 — white bread). The input record values (energy 118.18 kcal, protein 3.27 g, fat 6.91 g, carbohydrate 10.91 g per 100 g) are consistent with a soup-dominant composite at approximately 60% soup and 40% bread by weight and are retained as the best available estimate. Singapore HPB Foodo database was consulted but does not contain this specific dish. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are estimated from USDA component data.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — White bread GI reference (GI 71); adjusted downward to 62 for composite dish with soup component diluting bread GI.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cashew truffle mushroom soup bread bowl. The bread bowl component (white bread, GI ~71 per University of Sydney GI Database) dominates the carbohydrate load. The soup component (mushroom-based, low-GI ingredients: cashews GI ~25, mushrooms negligible GI) dilutes the overall GI. A weighted composite GI of approximately 62 is estimated based on carbohydrate contribution: bread bowl ~70% of total carbohydrate at GI 71, soup component ~30% at GI ~30. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI 62 × available carbohydrate per serving (60 g from 550 g at 10.91 g/100 g) / 100 = 37.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 172.7 mg per 100 g; full serving delivers approximately 950 mg sodium, which is moderate within a 2000 mg daily target
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request low-sodium vegetable broth and limit consumption of the bread bowl, which adds refined carbohydrate and additional sodium; consuming only the soup portion reduces sodium intake to approximately 520 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (~37) driven by the white bread bowl (GI ~71); total carbohydrate per 550 g serving is approximately 60 g with dietary fibre only 7 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid the bread bowl and request the soup served in a regular bowl, which reduces the carbohydrate load from approximately 60 g to under 20 g per serving and lowers the glycemic impact substantially.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.45 g per 100 g; cholesterol is 0 mg; cashews and truffle oil (olive-oil base) provide predominantly monounsaturated fatty acids beneficial for lipid profiles
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is broadly suitable for cardiovascular health due to its low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and MUFA-rich fat profile from cashews and olive-oil-based truffle oil; patients should limit the bread bowl portion to control refined carbohydrate intake and monitor total sodium from broth.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory MUFA from olive-oil-based truffle oil and beta-glucans from mushrooms; very low saturated fat at 1.45 g per 100 g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The mushroom and truffle oil components provide anti-inflammatory compounds; patients should choose a wholemeal bread bowl or omit the bowl entirely to avoid refined carbohydrate-driven low-grade inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cashew Truffle Mushroom Soup Bread Bowl is a plant-based, vegan-friendly composite dish consisting of a rich, velvety mushroom soup enriched with blended cashews for creaminess and finished with truffle oil for an earthy, aromatic depth, served inside a hollowed-out baked bread bowl. The soup is prepared by sautéing mushrooms, onion, and garlic in olive oil, simmering in vegetable broth, then blending with soaked cashews to achieve a smooth, dairy-free cream texture. A few drops of truffle oil are added at the end to preserve the volatile aromatic compounds. The bread bowl is typically made from white or wholemeal bread dough baked into a round loaf, then hollowed out to serve as an edible vessel. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 118 kcal, 3.3 g protein, 6.9 g fat (predominantly monounsaturated from cashews and olive oil), and 10.9 g carbohydrate. Sodium is moderate at 173 mg per 100 g. A full 550 g serving delivers approximately 650 kcal and 60 g carbohydrate, making glycemic management the primary clinical concern. The dish is suitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease at moderate portion sizes but is high-concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the high-GI bread bowl component. It is free from dairy and cholesterol, making it appropriate for vegan and lactose-intolerant consumers. The dish is popular in Singapore café and casual dining settings and is available through food delivery platforms. Clinically, patients with diabetes are advised to request the soup without the bread bowl to substantially reduce carbohydrate and glycemic load.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by component weighting using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cream of mushroom soup and white bread, adjusted for cashew cream substitution. The input record values from GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with this estimation and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database white bread reference. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is robust and the composite estimation methodology is defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: cream of mushroom soup (ID 06037), raw cashews (ID 12087), and white bread (ID 18069). No exact approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Singapore HPB Foodo database was consulted and does not contain this specific dish. 2. Monounsaturated fat (3.20 g/100 g) and polyunsaturated fat (1.10 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA component data for cashews and olive oil; these are not directly reported in the input record. 3. Potassium (195 mg/100 g) is estimated from mushroom and cashew USDA component data. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient breakdown for this composite dish preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as a single composite entry; component-level estimation for these micronutrients was not performed due to insufficient preparation detail. 5. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg per 100 g: confirmed as naturally zero for this fully plant-based vegan dish containing no animal-derived ingredients. 6. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. The ethnic_tags in the input (Ramen, Pizza, Pasta) appear erroneous for this dish and have been removed. 8. Trans fat (0.04 g/100 g) is retained from the input record; this trace level is consistent with naturally occurring trans fats in plant oils at very low levels.
